metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC2's oracle to assert structural identity (e.g., `assert plan_sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review_sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` or that both modules import from the same source), not merely value equality, so the test actually proves 'one named constant' rather than 'matching integers'.
evidence: AC2 text: 'The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths'
AC2 oracle: 'a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value' — value equality does not prove single-constant governance
findings.finding: AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') only verifies value-equality (both == 50), not structural sharing. It would pass even with mirrored constants, which contradicts AC2's own text ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths').
scenarios: Both files define `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` independently; the oracle test passes; one file is later changed to 40; CI catches nothing until a prune test fails.
A structural import test (asserting both modules reference the same object) would catch this; a value-equality test would not.
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC2 oracle to: 'a test importing both sidecar modules and asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (object identity), or asserting both reference the same fully-qualified constant from a shared module.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and update the plan to explicitly state that `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` requires a NEW `prune()` function and a wiring change in its `emit()`, since neither currently exists — 'ensure the same cap governs' implies an existing prune path that the file read refutes.
evidence: Plan scope: 'code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant), so both paths retain identically.'

grep for 'RETAIN_PER_TICKET' across src/rebar/llm/code_review/ returns zero matches
Full read of src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py confirms: no prune() function, no retention constant, emit() does not call prune

findings.finding: The plan asserts a code-review prune path already exists in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` that can be updated to share the `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant, but the file contains no `prune()` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — the emit() function never calls prune. The plan's scope says 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)', treating the prune path as pre-existing, but it must be created from scratch.
scenarios: The implementer reads 'ensure the same cap governs' as a one-line constant change and ships without adding a prune() call in code_review/sidecar.py — code-review REVIEW_RESULT events are never pruned, growth stays unbounded.

AC2's oracle test ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') cannot pass without a callable prune path in code_review/sidecar.py — the test would have nothing to invoke on the code-review side.

findings.finding: The plan asserts there is an existing 'code-review prune path' in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` that needs only a constant update, but the file contains no `prune()` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — the prune path must be written from scratch and wired into `emit()`.

findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py contains no prune() function and no RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant — the 'code-review prune path' the plan names as an existing edit target does not exist; wiring it in requires creating both the function and a call from emit(), which is new behavior (CREATION), not an update.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Scope entry for code_review/sidecar.py to read: 'Add RETAIN_PER_TICKET (shared with or mirrored from plan_review/sidecar.py) and a prune() function modelled on plan_review/sidecar.prune(); wire it as a best-effort call in emit() after append_event — mirroring the plan-review emit() pattern.'

findings.finding: The code_review/sidecar.py file has no prune() function and its emit() makes no prune call — there is no 'code-review prune path' to govern with a shared or mirrored constant. The plan's mechanism for the code-review half (add/share the constant) is incomplete: a constant with no calling code does not bound code-review sidecar growth.
findings.location: What / Scope section — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune'
findings.suggested_fix: In code_review/sidecar.py: (1) define RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 (or import it from plan_review.sidecar); (2) add a prune() function identical in structure to plan_review/sidecar.prune() filtering on f"-{EVENT_TYPE}.json"; (3) call prune(target_ticket, repo_root=repo_root) at the end of emit() (best-effort, exception-swallowed), mirroring line 63 of plan_review/sidecar.py.

findings.finding: The code-review sidecar (`src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`) has no `prune` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — confirmed by tool search. The plan's Scope says 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune' and AC2 oracles 'a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value,' treating a prune path as already existing. It does not exist. The plan therefore fails to specify: (1) that a new `prune()` function must be authored for this file, (2) where in `emit()` it should be called, and (3) whether it should follow the same best-effort/exception-swallowing convention as the plan-review counterpart.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for `code_review/sidecar.py`: 'Add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` function mirroring `plan_review/sidecar.prune` (same git-rm logic, same best-effort/exception-swallowing pattern) and call it from `emit()` immediately after the `append_event` call, returning `True` on success regardless of prune outcome.'

findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') is weaker than AC2's stated criterion ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths'). A mirrored-constants implementation (two separate variables both set to 50) satisfies the oracle but violates the 'one named constant' structural claim, meaning the AC can be green while the criterion is false.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths. Oracle: a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 oracle: 'a test asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (import identity), confirming a single definition site; plus `grep -c "RETAIN_PER_TICKET\s*=" src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` should equal 1 (only one assignment exists in the codebase).'

findings.finding: The plan's Scope and AC2 claim a 'code-review prune path' exists in `code_review/sidecar.py` and needs its constant aligned — but that file has neither a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant nor a `prune()` function. Proposing a 'shared constant governs both prune paths' when one path doesn't yet exist is premature abstraction; AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') would require adding new production code that the plan never acknowledges as a new addition.
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune') and AC2 ('The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)' with an explicit statement: 'Add a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` constant and a `prune()` call in `code_review/sidecar.py`'s `emit()`, mirroring the plan-review sidecar pattern.' Then update AC2's oracle to describe the new test accordingly. Alternatively, if code-review growth is not a current concern, remove the code-review prune from scope entirely and narrow AC2 to plan-review only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Scope whether the code-review prune path needs to be CREATED (add `prune()` + call from `emit()` in `code_review/sidecar.py`) or whether the constant is being mirrored for future use only, and update AC2's oracle to match whichever is intended.
evidence: grep of code_review/sidecar.py: zero hits for 'RETAIN_PER_TICKET' and zero hits for 'prune'

findings.finding: The plan's Scope and AC2 both reference a 'code-review prune path,' but code_review/sidecar.py currently has no RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant and no prune() function — its emit() never calls prune. The plan does not explicitly state whether it will ADD a prune() to code_review/sidecar.py or merely share/mirror the constant for a future prune path; either interpretation leaves AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') ambiguous or unsatisfiable without undeclared scope.
findings.location: Scope section ('code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune') and AC2 ('The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths')
scenarios: Implementer adds only the constant to code_review/sidecar.py without a prune() → AC2's oracle test cannot be written in any meaningful way (no prune path to invoke).
Implementer adds a full prune() to code_review/sidecar.py → undeclared scope; the plan's Scope section does not mention this work, risking an unreviewed prune implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is a genuine shared cap: add one sentence to Scope — 'Add `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` to `code_review/sidecar.py` and call it from `emit()`, mirroring the plan_review pattern.' If the intent is constant-only for now, rewrite AC2 to: 'The constant in `code_review/sidecar.py` is imported from or numerically matches `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`; oracle: a unit test asserting their equality.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve 'shared or mirrored' ambiguity in Scope: state explicitly whether `code_review/sidecar.py` should *import* `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `plan_review/sidecar.py` (or a shared module) or define its own copy, and update the Scope bullet to reflect that decision.
evidence: 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)'
'A single shared constant (rather than a new config key)' in Approach rationale implies shared import, yet the scope bullet hedges with 'or mirrored'
findings.finding: The Scope section says 'shared or mirrored constant' for the code-review path, leaving open two distinct implementation approaches (a single imported constant vs. two files each defining the same literal). These produce different structural guarantees and different test strategies, and the plan does not decide between them.
scenarios: Executor imports the constant from plan_review/sidecar.py into code_review/sidecar.py (shared), making future drift impossible.
Executor duplicates the literal 50 in code_review/sidecar.py (mirrored), satisfying AC2's value-equality oracle while allowing future divergence.
Two contributors choose different approaches, producing inconsistent codebase structure.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'shared or mirrored constant' with: 'import `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `plan_review/sidecar.py` (or extract to a shared `src/rebar/llm/review_constants.py`) so a single definition governs both paths.' This aligns with the Approach rationale's 'single shared constant' language.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mandate a single authoritative import (e.g. a shared `constants.py` or top-level `sidecar_constants.py`) rather than allowing a mirrored copy, so divergence is a module-not-found error, not a silent runtime difference.
evidence: "ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)" — 'mirrored' allows two independent definitions
AC2 test catches divergence at test time but not at authorship time; no import structure is mandated
findings.finding: The Scope section permits a 'mirrored constant' (two separate definitions with the same value) as an alternative to a truly shared import, while the What section says 'a single named constant governing both paths.' A mirrored constant has no compile-time or import-time enforcement of sameness; a future edit to one file could silently diverge the two caps, violating the invariant the AC test is meant to guard.
findings.location: Scope section — 'shared or mirrored constant'; What section — 'one named constant governing both'
scenarios: A developer edits plan_review/sidecar.py's local copy of the constant to 100 for a quick experiment and forgets to update code_review/sidecar.py — the two prune paths silently diverge; the AC test would catch it only if re-run.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `src/rebar/llm/review_constants.py` exporting `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50`; have both `plan_review/sidecar.py` and `code_review/sidecar.py` import from it. Remove the 'or mirrored constant' wording from Scope.

findings.finding: The codebase contains a third sidecar with its own RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py. The plan does not mention this sibling at all — neither to include it in scope nor to justify excluding it. This is pre-existing same-pattern prior art whose omission from the plan leaves a reader uncertain whether the 10→50 change is intentionally scoped to REVIEW_RESULT sidecars only.
scenarios: A developer reading the plan later wonders whether completion_sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET should also be 50 and whether it was an oversight; a reviewer raises the same question during code review, requiring a second PR or a plan amendment.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Out of scope: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py — COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars have a distinct use case (ephemeral FAIL diagnostics, not audit history) and are intentionally excluded from this retention increase.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
